canto_title: The Nature of Love and the Passing of the Great Crowd

scenes.summary: Virgil finishes his thought and notices Dante's reluctance to ask a question, prompting him to encourage Dante to speak.
Dante expresses his clarity of vision and asks Virgil to explain the nature of love and its opposite.
Virgil explains how the soul is naturally inclined to love whatever provides pleasure, driven by instinct and nature.
Virgil compares the upward movement of fire to the spiritual desire of the soul to reach the object of its love.
Virgil warns that while the essence of love may be good, the outward signs or 'wax' can often be misleading.
Dante responds that Virgil's explanation has increased his doubt regarding whether the soul is responsible for its own merit.
Virgil tells Dante that the answer to his doubt lies ahead with Beatrice, which is a matter of faith.
Virgil explains that every substantial form possesses an inherent virtue that is only revealed through action.
Virgil describes how the soul's first inclinations are instinctive, like a bee's drive to make honey, and are beyond praise or blame.
Virgil explains that the virtue of the soul lies in its ability to judge and accept or reject these innate inclinations.
Virgil notes that those who have reached the depths of reasoning understand this freedom, which is the foundation of morality.
Virgil concludes that while loves may arise from necessity, the power to retain or reject them resides in human free will.
The scene shifts to a description of the moon and the sparse appearance of the stars in the late night sky.
Dante describes his own physical state of heavy, drowsy exhaustion as he walks with Virgil.
Dante's sleepiness is interrupted by the sudden appearance of a large, rushing crowd of spirits.
Two figures in the crowd cry out about the historical conquests of Mary and Caesar.
Other members of the crowd urge their companions to hurry so that time is not wasted.
Virgil asks the rushing group for directions to the path that leads upward.
A spirit, formerly an Abbot from Verona, replies and provides a grim prophecy regarding the downfall of his monastery.
Dante notes how quickly the spirit passed by and expresses his desire to retain the message.
Virgil directs Dante to look at two other spirits who are being driven by sloth.
The departing spirits cry out biblical references regarding the Red Sea and the hardships of Dido.
As the spirits disappear, Dante's mind wanders through various thoughts until he eventually falls into a dream-state.
